metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a clause to the T5c rubric (and reflect it in AC1) clarifying that in a mixed-scope plan — one that introduces both boundary-crossing and in-process components — T5c sub-checks apply only to the boundary-crossing components; the in-process/local-CLI portions of the same plan remain not-applicable.
findings.finding: The trust-boundary gate is framed at plan-level ('when the plan introduces/exposes a component reachable by a lower-trust actor'), but does not specify how the agent should behave when a plan introduces BOTH boundary-crossing components AND purely local/in-process components. Without this, the implementer writing the rubric text — and future agents applying it — may either over-fire (treating in-process logic as a security gap because the plan also has a network surface) or scope ambiguously.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('a security finding is in scope only when the plan introduces/exposes a component reachable by a lower-trust actor')
findings.suggested_fix: In the revised T5c rubric text, after the leading gate sentence, add: 'Where a plan introduces both a boundary-crossing surface AND purely local/in-process components, apply each sub-check only to the boundary-crossing components; local/in-process components within the same plan are not-applicable even when the plan as a whole triggers the gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback rule to the T5c rubric for the ambiguous-reachability case (e.g. 'if the plan does not specify whether the component is network-reachable, treat as not-applicable and note the assumption in the finding rationale') so the AGENT's scope gate is fully deterministic.
findings.finding: The new trust-boundary reachability judgement ('in scope only when reachable by a lower-trust actor') has no specified fallback protocol for plans where reachability is ambiguous or unstated. The plan defines the positive and negative cases (clearly networked → in scope; clearly local/in-process → PASS) but leaves no decision rule for the common middle case where a plan neither confirms nor denies external reachability — precisely the class of plan (e.g. a library that might later gain a network wrapper) where false-positive risk is highest.
findings.location: AC1: trust-boundary scope gate definition; plan_review_T5c.md (target file)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a task to capture the trust-boundary-as-scope-gate decision (rationale, alternatives considered, T10 exclusion reasoning, carve-out logic) in an ADR or `CLAUDE.md` update so the design intent is durable beyond the ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — trust-boundary crossing as a first-class, generalized scope gate for security review — whose rationale (STRIDE Spoofing, OWASP ASVS 1.4.1, Semgrep/Anthropic exploitability-over-category, rejection of false-positive patterns) is captured only in the ticket context section, not in any persistent design record (ADR or CLAUDE.md update).
findings.location: Context section / plan_review_T5c.md (no ADR/CLAUDE.md update mentioned)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line: 'An ADR (or `CLAUDE.md` / `docs/decisions/` entry) records: (a) why trust-boundary crossing was chosen as the scope gate, (b) what alternatives were rejected, (c) why T10 is excluded from this framing, and (d) the positive-pass carve-out rationale for in-process/loopback surfaces.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the hedge in the plan: either (a) confirm `criteria_routing.json` T5c checklist items will be updated to match the trust-boundary framing and enumerate `tests/unit/test_criteria_overlay.py` + the validate-routing CI gate as affected consumers, or (b) explicitly state why the checklist items need no change and how parity is maintained.
findings.finding: The `criteria_routing.json` T5c checklist is a live execution artifact — its `access_classification` item currently reads 'Every new endpoint/path declares its access level; sensitive paths require auth (ambiguity about access level is itself the failure) — cite OWASP category', which is precisely the pre-trust-boundary framing the plan is replacing. If the `.md` rubric is updated but the JSON checklist is not, the checklist and rubric diverge; if the JSON IS updated, the `validate-routing` CI gate and `tests/unit/test_criteria_overlay.py` parity test (line 306) are unenumerated consumers that will be affected — neither is in the plan's Files list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact replacement text for the `access_classification` checklist key in `criteria_routing.json` so it mirrors the new trust-boundary gate (e.g. 'Security concern is in scope only when the component is reachable by a lower-trust actor; in-process / local-CLI / loopback-only → PASS not-applicable'), keeping the .md rubric and the routing checklist coherent.
findings.finding: The criteria_routing.json T5c checklist key 'access_classification' carries text that directly contradicts the proposed trust-boundary framing and is never specified to change. The plan says 'if needed' for criteria_routing.json, but the existing check ('Every new endpoint/path declares its access level; sensitive paths require auth — cite OWASP category') is exactly the generic-web-app import the new rubric is meant to eliminate as a false-positive source. After the plan executes, the .md rubric and the routing checklist will be incoherent: the AGENT reads the .md (trust-boundary gate), but the Pass-1 orchestrator surfaces the old checklist text to users and uses it for routing/display — a processing protocol split across two artifacts with no specified reconciliation.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json — T5c checklist key 'access_classification'; plan AC1 and plan 'Files' section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `access_classification` check text in criteria_routing.json T5c entry with: "A security finding is in scope only when the plan introduces/exposes a component reachable by a lower-trust actor (public internet, another tenant, untrusted network, unauthenticated user); in-process / local-CLI / loopback-only surfaces → PASS not-applicable." Then add the per-dimension sub-keys (authn, encryption, least-privilege, secrets) as conditional on boundary crossing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'LOWER severity' in the T5c rubric text with a concrete reference (e.g. 'flag as advisory rather than blocking' or 'severity: medium vs high for internet-reachable') so the AGENT has an unambiguous, schema-consistent instruction for the private-network case.
findings.finding: The zero-trust caveat in AC4 ('single-tenant / private network is LOWER severity, not exempt') introduces a severity-graduation rule with undefined output vocabulary. Neither the current plan_review_T5c.md, nor criteria_routing.json, nor any referenced schema defines the severity scale the AGENT must use when applying this rule. The AGENT will need to emit a severity-qualified finding for private-network surfaces, but the plan specifies no mapping from 'lower severity' to any concrete value in the finding schema.
findings.location: AC4: 'Zero-trust caveat encoded: a single-tenant / private network is LOWER severity, not exempt.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete artifact and location for the 'old-vs-new documented' sub-claim in AC 6 (e.g., 'append a `## Calibration` section to `plan_review_T5c.md` or a sibling `T5c_calibration.md` showing old vs. new verdicts for d251, 8a1c, 4702').
findings.finding: AC 6 ('old-vs-new documented') does not specify a concrete artifact or location for the documentation, making it impossible to verify done-ness.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'old-vs-new documented' with: 'results appended to `docs/calibration/T5c_calibration.md` (or inline in the PR description) showing the old verdict and new verdict for each fixture, with the proving command `cat docs/calibration/T5c_calibration.md` confirming the file exists and is non-empty'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan body where and in what form the old-vs-new T5c calibration results are documented (e.g., a committed `docs/validation/T5c_recalibration.md` or a required PR comment with before/after findings).
findings.finding: AC6 requires 'old-vs-new documented' as a distinct artifact but the plan body describes no step, location, or format for producing this documentation, leaving it as uncovered work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly require at least one live (non-stubbed) LLM run for each validation scenario (d251 positive, 8a1c/4702 negative) — OR add a documented rationale explaining why the offline/recorded substitute faithfully reflects the changed rubric's behavior.
findings.finding: The validation AC is satisfiable by replaying pre-existing canned/recorded LLM responses that pre-date the rubric change. Because the changed artifact is a prompt/rubric (plan_review_T5c.md), an offline test that feeds the NEW rubric to a canned-response LLM stub will produce the old output regardless of what the rubric now says — the suite goes green while the rubric change has no effect in the live path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Validation AC: 'recalibrated T5c run against d251 … run against a local no-network negative (8a1c/4702) PASSes as not-applicable — old-vs-new documented'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the validation AC: 'Validation runs MUST use a live LLM call (not replayed fixtures) so the changed rubric text is actually evaluated; record the new outputs as the updated baseline.' If cost/latency is a concern, scope to a single live acceptance run per scenario gated in CI.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC 6 whether the d251 validation run must be a live model execution or whether an offline/logged replay is acceptable, and document the rationale if offline is chosen.
findings.finding: AC 6 does not specify whether the T5c validation run against d251 uses a live model call or an offline/mocked execution, leaving the cutover path potentially unexercised end-to-end.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either commit to d251 as the canonical positive-case fixture, or define the minimum criteria for an 'equivalent' plan (e.g., 'exposes at least one HTTP endpoint reachable by unauthenticated public internet traffic') so the executor has no discretion to pick an under-powered substitute.
findings.finding: AC 6 uses 'or an equivalent networked-service plan' as a fallback for d251, deferring the choice of positive test fixture to the executor.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(or an equivalent networked-service plan)' with '(if d251 is unavailable, a plan that introduces a public HTTP endpoint with no authentication boundary — confirm with plan author before substituting)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact test file/name and proving command for the T10 non-regression assertion in AC 5 (e.g., 'existing test `tests/plan_review/test_T10_routing.py::test_endpoint_access_contract_scope` still passes after changes; verified via `pytest tests/plan_review/test_T10_routing.py`').
findings.finding: AC 5 ('a test/asserts T10 still fires only on infra intent') names no specific test file, test name, or proving command, so an executor cannot verify T10 non-regression without inferring what to write.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan body (and to the Files list) the specific test file(s) that will assert T10 `endpoint_access_contract` still fires only on infra intent, so AC5's test deliverable has a covered implementation path.
findings.finding: AC5 requires a concrete test artifact ('a test/asserts T10 still fires only on infra intent') but no corresponding work is described in the plan body and no test file is listed under Files; this deliverable is an orphan criterion with no implementation coverage.
findings.suggested_fix: Under Files, add the relevant test path (e.g. `tests/plan_review/test_T10_routing.py`). In the plan body, add a sentence describing: 'Add/extend a unit test that feeds a non-infra plan to T10 and asserts no `endpoint_access_contract` finding is emitted, and feeds an infra plan and asserts the finding IS emitted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the file (e.g. a test fixture or unit-test path) where the T10 non-regression assertion lives so AC5 has an end-state-observable proof location.
findings.finding: AC5 names a test/assertion that T10 still fires only on infra intent, but no file path or test location is listed in the plan's file scope, leaving this deliverable without an end-state-observable proof anchor.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files list: `tests/plan_review/test_t10_scope.py` (or equivalent), and update AC5 to read: 'A test in `<path>` asserts T10 fires on an infra-intent plan and does NOT fire on a T5c-only trust-boundary plan.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the Langfuse prompt `plan-review-T5c` as a required edit target alongside `plan_review_T5c.md`, or document explicitly how the `.md` change propagates to the live execution prompt.
findings.finding: The Langfuse prompt `plan-review-T5c` is the live execution copy of the T5c rubric (the `.md` is only a local fallback), but the plan names only the `.md` file as an edit target and does not enumerate the Langfuse deployment step. Changes to `plan_review_T5c.md` that are not also pushed to the Langfuse prompt library will not take effect in production runs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `plan-review-T5c` (Langfuse prompt) to the plan's Files list with a note that the local `.md` change must be pushed to Langfuse (e.g. via the prompt-authoring workflow or `rebar prompt push`) as part of the same work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py` to the plan's Files list (or confirm it needs no changes) given it contains T5c-specific `applies()` assertions that are sensitive to routing-metadata edits.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_plan_review.py` contains a dedicated T5c assertion block (lines 385–388) that verifies T5c's `applies()` behavior on containers vs. leaves. If the trust-boundary reframe alters T5c's routing metadata in `criteria_routing.json` (e.g. `applies_at` scope), this test is an unenumerated consumer that may need updating; it is absent from the plan's Files list.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
